CHAPTER 1.08
COMPLAINTS COMMISSIONER (OMBUDSMAN) ORDINANCE
(Ordinances 12 of 2015 and 10 of 2017)
AN ORDINANCE TO PROVIDE FOR THE OFFICE OF THE COMPLAINTS
COMMISSIONER; TO MAKE PROVISION FOR THE APPOINTMENT, FUNCTIONS AND
JURISDICTION OF THE COMPLAINTS COMMISSIONER; FOR THE INVESTIGATION OF
ADMINISTRATIVE ACTION TAKEN ON BEHALF OF THE GOVERNMENT AND CERTAIN
BODIES; AND FOR CONNECTED PURPOSES.
Commencement
[1 August 2015]
PART I
PRELIMINARY
Short title
1. This Ordinance may be cited as the Complaints Commissioner
(Ombudsman) Ordinance.
Interpretation
2. (1) In this Ordinance—
“administrative action” means any action taken by or on behalf of an authority in
the exercise of its administrative functions and includes—
(a)a decision or an act;
(b) a failure to make a decision or do an act, including a failure to
provide reasons for a decision;
(c)a recommendation;
(d)any action taken because of a recommendation; or
(e)a failure to make a recommendation;
“authority’ means a government department or other body to which this
Ordinance applies;
“Commission” means the office of the Complaints Commissioner, including the
Complaints Commissioner and the staff;
“Commissioner” means the Complaints Commissioner whose appointment is
referred to in section 4;
“complaint” means a complaint made under this Ordinance;
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

6 CAP. 1.08 Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) LAWS OF TURKS &
CAICOS ISLANDS
Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
“complainant” means a person aggrieved by maladministration or a person who
makes a complaint under this Ordinance;
“Court” means the Magistrate’s Court;
“investigation” means an investigation under this Ordinance;
“maladministration” means inefficient, bad or improper administration, and
includes—
(a) unreasonable delay in dealing with the subject matter of an
investigation;
(b)abuse of any power (including any discretionary power); or
(c)administrative action that was—
(i) contrary to law;
(ii) unfair, oppressive or improperly discriminatory or based on
procedures that are unfair, oppressive or improperly
discriminatory;
(iii) based wholly or partly on a mistake of law or fact or
irrelevant grounds;
(iv) related to the application of arbitrary or unreasonable
procedures; or
(v) negligent;
“officer” includes an employee.
(2) Reference to a government department, authority or body of persons
includes a reference to Ministers, members or officers of that department,
authority or body of persons.
(3) Nothing in this Ordinance authorises or requires the Commissioner to
question the merits of a decision taken without maladministration by an authority
in the exercise of a decision vested in that authority.
Application
3. (1) This Ordinance applies to—
(a) government departments;
(b) government boards;
(c) statutory bodies; and
(d) any other corporation or body—
(i) established by an Ordinance; or
(ii) whose revenues are appropriated by the House of Assembly,
or whose fees or charges of any other description are
authorised by the House of Assembly.
(2) This Ordinance does not apply to the persons and bodies listed in
Schedule 1.

Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

PART II
OFFICE OF COMMISSIONER, FUNCTIONS AND JURISDICTION
Office of Complaints Commissioner
4. (1) The Complaints Commissioner is appointed in terms of section 99 of
the Constitution.
(2) The Commissioner may appoint such officers as are required for the
proper performance of the functions of the Commission.
(3) The Commissioner may in addition, engage from time to time,
technical or professional advisors as he considers necessary to assist in the
discharge of his functions.
(4) A function of the Commissioner under this Ordinance may be
performed by an officer of the Commission authorised for that purpose by the
Commissioner.
Oath of secrecy
5. The Commissioner and an officer of the Commission shall, before
assuming the functions of his office, make and subscribe to the oath of secrecy in
the Form set out in Schedule 2.
Funds, accounts and audit
6. (1) The funds of the Commission shall consist of funds provided to the
Commission by or under any appropriation law.
(2) The Commission shall keep proper accounts of all its transactions to
the satisfaction of the Permanent Secretary, Finance and in a form that conforms
to internationally recognised standards.
(3) The Commission shall have regard to and comply with its obligation
to prepare and submit its annual budget and its financial statements for auditing
within the timeframe required under the Public Finance Management Ordinance
and National Audit Ordinance.
(4) Section 44 of the Public Finance Management Ordinance shall apply
in relation to the unexpended balance of moneys appropriated to the Commission
for any financial year. (Inserted by Ord. 10 of 2017)
Functions and jurisdiction
7. (1) The functions of the Commissioner are—
(a) to investigate an administrative action of an authority for the
purpose of deciding whether there is evidence of
maladministration on the part of the authority;
(b) following an investigation, to make recommendations to the
authority concerning an administrative action that formed the
subject of the investigation and, generally, to recommend ways of
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

8 CAP. 1.08 Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) L

Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
improving the authority’s administrative practices and
procedures; and
(c) to perform such other powers as may be conferred on him under
this Ordinance or any other Ordinance.
(2) Subject to this Ordinance, the Commissioner may investigate any
administrative action taken by or on behalf of an authority—
(a) where a complaint is made by a person who claims to have been
treated unjustly as a result of maladministration arising from or in
connection with the administrative action taken by the authority;
or
(b) on his own motion, where he—
(i) considers that he ought to investigate any matter on the
ground that a person or body of persons has or may have
sustained an injustice as a result of maladministration; or
(ii) is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to carry out an
investigation in the public interest.
(3) Subject to this Ordinance, in determining whether to initiate, continue
or discontinue an investigation, the Commissioner shall act in his own discretion
and any question whether a complaint is duly made under this Ordinance shall be
determined by the Commissioner.
Restrictions on matters for investigation
8. (1) The Commissioner shall not conduct an investigation on any matter—
(a) where the complainant has or had a right of appeal, reference or
review to or before a tribunal constituted by or under any
Ordinance;
(b) where the complainant has had or had a remedy by way of
proceedings in any court; or
(c) set out in Schedule 3.
(2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), the Commissioner may investigate a
matter although the complainant has or had a right or remedy before a tribunal or
by way of proceedings in any court, if the Commissioner is satisfied that in the
particular circumstances it is not reasonable to expect the complainant to take or
to have taken such proceedings.
(3) The Commissioner shall not investigate—
(a) a matter which relates to corruption and falls within the powers of
the Integrity Commission; or
(b) a matter which falls within the powers of the Human Rights
Commission.
(4) The Commissioner shall—

Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.

(a) on receipt of a complaint which it is evident falls within the
powers referred to under subsection (3), refer the complainant to
the relevant authority; or
(b) in the course of the investigation, where it becomes apparent that
the complaint falls within the powers referred to under subsection
(3), transfer the matter to the relevant authority.
PART III
COMPLAINTS AND INVESTIGATION PROCESS
Making a complaint
9. (1) A complaint to the Commissioner about an administrative action of an
authority shall be made—
(a) by an aggrieved individual, but may be made on behalf of that
individual by a member of his family or other suitable person if
the individual by whom the complaint is to be made is dead or
unable to act for himself; or
(b) by a body of persons whether incorporated or not, not being—
(i) a body constituted for purposes of the public service or of
Government, or for the purposes of carrying on any industry
or undertaking or part of an industry or undertaking under
national ownership; or
(ii) any other authority or body whose members are appointed by
the Government or a government department, or whose
revenues consist wholly or mainly of moneys provided by
Government.
(2) A complaint—
(a) may be made orally, electronically or in writing; and
(b) shall be made within one year from the day the complainant first
had notice of the matters alleged in the complaint.
(3) A complaint made orally shall be reduced in writing by the officer
taking the complaint, and shall be read to the complainant who shall attest his
signature to the complaint.
(4) Notwithstanding subsection (2)(b), the Commissioner may conduct
an investigation not made within the period specified in that subsection if he
considers that there are special circumstances which make it proper to do so.
(5) Where a complaint is made to the Commissioner, he shall record—
(a) the complainant’s name, address and telephone number;
(b) the subject matter of the complaint; and
(c) the date when the complaint was made.
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

(6) A person who is detained in custody or otherwise confined in an
institution may make a complaint by informing the person in charge or another
person performing the duties in connection with his detention or confinement,
that he wishes to make a complaint to the Commissioner.
(7) A person informed of the intention to make a complaint under
subsection (6) shall—
(a) take all necessary steps to facilitate the making of the complaint
and shall provide an unsealed envelope to the person in custody
or otherwise confined; and
(b) without delay, after receiving the envelope from the person in
custody or otherwise confined, seal the envelope and send it
sealed to the Commissioner.
(8) A communication from the Commissioner to a person in custody or
otherwise confined shall be forwarded to that person in a sealed envelope.
(9) The Commissioner shall write to a complainant acknowledging
receipt of the complaint.
Preliminary inquiries
10. For the purposes of determining whether to undertake an investigation,
the Commissioner may conduct preliminary inquiries as he considers
appropriate.
When not to investigate
11. (1) A complaint shall not be investigated unless the complaint relates to
an action—
(a) taken in relation to a person while he was present in the Islands,
on a ship or aircraft registered in the Islands; or
(b) in relation to rights or obligations which accrued or arose in the
Islands or on such ship or aircraft.
(2) The Commissioner may decide not to investigate a complaint if he is
satisfied that—
(a) the complainant knew of the administrative action complained
against more than one year before the date when the
Commissioner received the complaint;
(b) the law or existing administrative procedure provides a remedy
adequate in the circumstances for the complainant and, if the
complainant has not availed himself of the remedy, there is no
reasonable justification for the failure to do so;
(c) where the complaint is made on behalf of a body corporate, the
body corporate has not authorised the making of the complaint;
(d) the subject matter of the complaint is trivial; or
(e) the complaint is frivolous, vexatious or not made in good faith.
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

& Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) CAP.1.08 11
Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
(3) The Commissioner may decide not to continue to investigate a
complaint if—
(a) the complainant has abandoned the complaint—
(i) by failing to advise the Commissioner of a current address or
a telephone number at which the Commissioner can contact
him; or
(ii) by failing to respond after a reasonable number of attempts by
the Commissioner to contact him;
(b) the complainant withdraws the complaint; or
(c) the complaint is settled or is successfully dealt with by mediation.
(4) Where the Commissioner decides not to investigate or continue to
investigate a complaint, he shall notify the complainant and a senior officer of
the authority of his decision in writing, and give reasons for the decision.
(5) The Commissioner may indicate in the notice under subsection (4) any
other recourse that may be available to the complainant.
Mediation
12. (1) The Commissioner may decide to deal with a complaint by mediation
if he is of the opinion, having regard to all the circumstances of the case, that
mediation is suitable in such circumstances.
(2) The Commissioner may appoint a person as a mediator for purposes
of this Ordinance.
(3) The Commissioner shall not participate in a mediation.
(4) Participation in a mediation by the authority that is the subject of the
investigation and the complainant is voluntary, and any party may withdraw at
any time.
(5) The mediator may decide to terminate a mediation at any time and,
when he does, he shall give reasons for his decision.
(6) If an attempt to deal with a complaint by mediation under this section is
unsuccessful—
(a) the complaint is to be treated as if the mediation had not taken
place; and
(b) the mediator is excluded from participating as an investigating
officer in any subsequent investigation of the complaint.
(7) Anything said or admitted during a mediation and any document
prepared for the purposes of such mediation—
(a) shall not be admissible in evidence in any subsequent
investigation of the complaint that is the subject of the
investigation unless the person who said or admitted the thing, or
to whom the document related, consents to its admission; and
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.

(b) shall not be admissible in evidence against a person in any court
or at any inquiry or in any other proceedings,
and no evidence in respect of the mediation may be given against any person.
(8) The costs of mediation shall be borne by the Commission.
Notice of intention to investigate
13. (1) If the Commissioner decides to conduct an investigation he shall,
before commencing an investigation, give a senior officer of the authority that is
the subject of a complaint, and the complainant, notice in writing of the
decision.
(2) The notice under subsection (1) shall—
(a) inform the senior officer and the complainant of the
Commissioner’s intention to conduct the investigation;
(b) specify the administrative action that is the subject of the
investigation;
(c) in general terms, inform the senior officer and the complainant of
the powers of the Commissioner regarding the investigation; and
(d) give the senior officer an opportunity to comment on any
allegations contained in the administrative action within fourteen
days of receipt of the notice.
Conduct of investigation
14. (1) Subject to this Ordinance, the Commissioner may regulate
investigations and proceedings in the manner that he sees fit.
(2) Every investigation shall be conducted in private, except in
circumstances where the Commissioner may determine another procedure as he
considers appropriate in any particular case.
(3) When conducting an investigation, the Commissioner may—
(a) obtain information from any person, and in the manner, he
considers appropriate;
(b) make such inquiries as he considers appropriate;
(c) determine whether a person may be represented by his attorney or
otherwise; and
(d) on giving reasonable notice to a senior officer in the authority
subject to the investigation, and at a reasonable time—
(i) enter the premises occupied by the authority for inspection;
and
(ii) subject to sections 15 and 16, carry out in those premises, any
investigation which is within his jurisdiction.
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

& Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) CAP.1.08 13
Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
(4) The Commissioner may, if he thinks fit, pay to the complainant or to
any other person who attends or furnishes information for purposes of an
investigation—
(a) sums in respect of expenses properly incurred by them; or
(b) compensation for the loss of their time,
in such amounts and subject to such conditions as may be determined by the
Government.
(5) If, during the course of an investigation or after an investigation, the
Commissioner is of the opinion that there is evidence of a breach of duty or
misconduct, or of an offence on the part of an officer of an authority, the
Commissioner shall refer the matter to an appropriate authority for further
consideration.
(6) Where the Commissioner makes reference to an appropriate authority
under subsection (5), proceedings under this Ordinance in respect to any
investigation are stayed pending consideration of the matter by the appropriate
authority.
(7) The conduct of an investigation under this Ordinance shall not affect
any action taken by the appropriate authority, or any power or duty of that
authority to take further action concerning any matters subject to investigation.
(8) Where a complainant has been removed from the Islands under the
Immigration Ordinance or another enactment, he shall, if the Commissioner
directs, be permitted to re-enter and remain in the Islands for the purposes of the
investigation, subject to such conditions as the Governor may direct.
Evidence
15. (1) For purposes of an investigation, the Commissioner—
(a) may require a Minister, officer or member of the authority subject
to investigation, complainant or any other person who in the
Commissioner’s opinion is able to provide information or
produce documents relevant to the investigation, to provide such
information or produce such documents;
(b) may summon before him and examine on oath or affirmation a
person referred to in paragraph (a); and
(c) shall have the same powers as the Court in so far as those powers
relate to the attendance and examination of witnesses and in
respect of the production of documents.
(2) The Commissioner shall not require—
(a) a person to provide information or answer any question relating to
proceedings or deliberations of Cabinet or any committee of
Cabinet;
(b) a person to produce any document as relates to proceedings in
paragraph (a) or that might prejudice the matters mentioned in
such proceedings; or
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.

(c) a member of Cabinet to provide information or answer any
question.
(3) For purposes of subsection (2), a certificate issued by the Governor
and certifying that any information, question or part of a document relates to the
proceedings shall be conclusive.
(4) Subsection (2)(c) shall not apply where a member of Cabinet is the
person being investigated and—
(a) the action in respect of which the complaint is made was taken in
pursuance of a decision of Cabinet; and
(b) the document or information in question is required by the
member of Cabinet to establish the true facts.
(5) Subject to subsection (2), a person shall not be compelled to give
evidence or produce any document which he could not be compelled to give or
produce in civil proceedings before the Court.
Protection and privileges of witnesses
16. (1) Every person shall have the same privileges in relation to—
(a) the giving of information to the Commissioner; and
(b) the production of documents and things to the Commissioner,
as witnesses have in the Court.
(2) Compliance with any requirement of the Commissioner under section
15—
(a) is not a breach of any relevant obligation of secrecy or non-
disclosure, or of the enactment or provision by which that
obligation is imposed; and
(b) shall not make a person liable to prosecution for an offence
against any enactment by reason only of that person’s compliance
with any requirement of the Commissioner under that section.
(3) Except in proceedings for an offence under section 19(2), 22 or 23—
(a) no statement made or answer given by any person in the course of
any investigation by or proceedings before the Commissioner
shall be admissible in evidence against that person or any other
person in any court or in any inquiry or other proceeding; and
(b) no evidence in respect of proceedings before the Commissioner
shall be given against any person.
Procedure after investigation
17. (1) If, at the conclusion of an investigation, the Commissioner determines
that there is no evidence of maladministration on the part of the authority that is
the subject of the investigation, the Commissioner shall record his decision and
the reasons in writing and, as soon as is reasonable, notify the complainant and
the senior officer of an authority of the decision.
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.
Printed under Authority by The Regional Law Revision Centre Inc.
www.lawrevision.ai

& Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) CAP.1.08 15
Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
(2) The Commissioner may indicate in the notice under subsection (1)
any other recourse that may be available to the complainant.
(3) If, at the conclusion of an investigation, the Commissioner decides
that there is evidence of maladministration on the part of the authority, the
Commissioner shall report his decision to the senior officer of the authority and
the complainant and make such recommendation to the authority as he sees fit.
(4) Without limiting subsection (3), the Commissioner may recommend
that—
(a) a matter should be referred to an appropriate authority for further
consideration;
(b) an omission or a delay should be rectified;
(c) a decision or recommendation should be cancelled or altered;
(d) reasons should be given;
(e) a practice, procedure or course of conduct should be altered; or
(f) an enactment should be reviewed.
(5) For the purposes of the law of defamation, a publication listed below
shall be absolutely privileged—
(a) the publication of any matter by the Commissioner in making a
report to the Governor, Cabinet or House of Assembly for the
purposes of this Ordinance;
(b) the publication of any matter by the Governor or a Member in
communicating with the Commissioner or his officers in
communicating with the Governor or such Member for those
purposes;
(c) the publication by the Governor or such Member to the person by
whom a complaint was made under this Ordinance of a report or
statement sent to the Member in respect of the complaint in
pursuance of subsection (1);
(d) the publication by the Commissioner to the person mentioned in
subsection (3), of a report to that person in pursuance of that
subsection.
(6) For the purposes of this section “Member” means a member of the
House of Assembly.
Authority to notify Commissioner of steps taken
18. (1) Where the Commissioner makes a recommendation to an authority
under section 17(4)—
(a) the authority shall within twenty business days of receipt of the
recommendation, notify the Commissioner, in writing, of the
action that has been taken or is proposed to give effect to the
recommendation; or
© Crown Copyright 2018
Copying/unauthorised distribution strictly prohibited.

(b) where the authority has taken no action within twenty business
days of receipt of the recommendation or does not propose to take
any action, the authority shall give reasons, in writing, for failure
to implement the recommendation.
(2) The Commissioner may, if he thinks fit in the circumstances of a
particular investigation, in writing, extend the period specified in subsection (1).
(3) If within the period specified in subsection (1) or (2), the authority—
(a) fails to notify the Commissioner of the action that has been taken
or is proposed; or
(b) has taken no action, or has taken action that in the Commissioner’s
opinion is inadequate or inappropriate,
the Commissioner, after considering any reasons given by the authority, may
submit a special report under section 21(2).
PART IV
MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS
Duty as to secrecy
19. (1) The Commissioner and every officer of the Commission and every
person performing a function in the service of the Commission shall treat all
records, and information relating to any complaint, as secret and confidential and
shall not disclose or communicate the text of any record, or information or,
complaint to an unauthorised person or allow an unauthorised person to have
access to any records, information or declarations.
(2) A person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and is
liable on summary conviction to a fine of $20,000 or to imprisonment for a term
of three years, or to both.
Immunity
20. (1) No action, suit, prosecution or proceeding shall be instituted against
the Commissioner or an officer of the Commission, for any act done or omitted
to be done in good faith in the exercise or discharge of any duty or function of
the Commissioner.
(2) The Commissioner or an officer of the Commission shall not be called
to give evidence in court or in any proceedings of a judicial nature, in respect of
anything coming to their knowledge in the exercise of their functions under this
Ordinance.
(3) Anything said or any information given or any document or thing
produced by a person in the course of an investigation conducted by, or
proceedings before the Commissioner under this Ordinance shall be privileged in
the same manner as if the investigation or proceedings were proceedings in the
Court.

& Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) CAP.1.08 17
Revision Date: 31 Mar 2018
Annual and special reports
21. (1) The Commissioner shall, as soon as practicable, and in any case within
six months after the end of each year, prepare a report on the performance of his
functions under this Ordinance during that year.
(2) Where—
(a) an administrative action that is under investigation is in the
opinion of the Commissioner, of public interest; or
(b) the Commissioner has made a recommendation under section
17(4) and within the period specified no action, or in his opinion
no adequate action has been taken by the authority to remedy the
administrative action complained against,
the Commissioner may prepare a special report on the investigation.
(3) The Commissioner shall submit to Cabinet, the special report
prepared under subsection (2).
(4) The Commissioner shall annually submit to the House of Assembly a
general report on the performance of his functions under this Ordinance, and
may from time to time submit to the House of Assembly, such other reports with
respect to his functions.
(5) Where any part of a report deals with any matter concerning an
authority for which a Minister is charged with responsibility, the Commissioner
shall also send copies of that part of the report to that Minister for his
information.
Obstruction and contempt
22. (1) If a person without lawful excuse obstructs the Commissioner or an
officer of the Commission in the performance of his functions under this
Ordinance, or is guilty of an act or omission in relation to any investigation
under this Ordinance which, if that investigation were a proceeding in the Court,
would constitute contempt of court, the Commissioner may certify the offence to
the Court.
(2) Where an offence is certified under this section, the Court may inquire
into the matter and, after hearing any witnesses who may be produced against or
on behalf of the person charged with the offence, and after hearing any statement
that may be offered in defence, deal with him in any manner in which the Court
could deal with him if he had committed a similar offence in relation to the
Court.
(3) Nothing in this section shall be construed as applying to the taking of
an action mentioned in section 14(7).
Offences
23. (1) A person commits an offence if he intentionally makes a false
statement to, misleads or attempts to mislead the Commissioner or another
person in the exercise of any function or power conferred by this Ordinance.

Amendment of Schedules
24. The Governor may by Order published in the Gazette, amend a Schedule.
Regulations
25. The Governor may make regulations prescribing anything under this
Ordinance which is to be prescribed or which is necessary or convenient to be
prescribed for the better carrying out of the objects and purposes of this
Ordnance, or to give force and effect to its provisions.

& Complaints Commissioner (Ombudsman) CAP.1.08 19

SCHEDULE 1
(Section 3(2))
PERSONS AND BODIES NOT SUBJECT TO INVESTIGATION
1. Governor
2. Cabinet
3. House of Assembly
4. Chief Justice and any Judge of the Supreme Court or Court of Appeal
5. Magistrate
6. Police
7. National Audit Office and Staff
__________
SCHEDULE 2
(Section 5)
OATH OF SECRECY
I,............................................................ being appointed Complaints Commissioner/officer of the
Complaints Commission do swear that I will not on any account or at any time disclose any
opinion, advice or privileged information of the Complaints Commission and that I will not,
except with the authority of the [Supreme Court or the Complaints Commissioner], and to such
extent as may be required for the good management of the affairs of the Commission, directly or
indirectly reveal the business or the proceedings of the Complaints Commission or the nature or
any contents of any document.
*If the Oath is taken by an officer, the provision for revealing or disclosing the business or
proceedings of the Commission will be on the authority of either the Supreme Court or the
Complaints Commissioner; if the Oath is taken by the Complaints Commissioner it will be on
authority of the Supreme Court.

SCHEDULE 3
(Section 8(1)(c))
MATTERS NOT SUBJECT TO INVESTIGATION
1. Action taken in matters certified by the Governor to affect relations or dealings between the
Government of the Turks and Caicos Islands and any other Government or international
organisation of States or Governments.
2. Action taken under any law relating to extradition or fugitive offenders.
3. Action taken by or with authority of the Attorney General, Director of Public Prosecutions,
Commissioner of Police, Director of Immigration or the Collector of Customs for the
purposes of investigating crime or of protecting the security of the Islands, including action
taken with respect to passports.
4. The commencement or conduct of civil or criminal proceedings before any court of law in
the Islands, or of proceedings before an international court or tribunal.
5. Action taken in respect of appointments or removals, pay, discipline, or other personal
matters other than superannuation, in relation to—
(a) service in any office or employment under the Government; or
(b) service in any office or employment, or under any contract for services, in
respect of which power to take action, or to determine or approve the action to
be taken, in such matters is vested in the Government.
